problem_statement: KanbanBoard.tsx is an oversized monolithic component that handles board layout, column rendering, card rendering, drag-and-drop logic, and state management in a single file. This density makes the core user-facing component fragile and difficult to maintain. Decomposition isolates each concern into a focused module that can be understood and tested independently.
in_scope: Analyse KanbanBoard.tsx to identify distinct responsibilities and logical boundaries
Split KanbanBoard.tsx into focused child components and/or utility modules (e.g. KanbanColumn, KanbanCard, drag-drop handlers)
Use 200 LOC as a preferred target for resulting modules but do not force artificial splits where readability would worsen
Ensure the decomposed components compose to produce identical behaviour to the original KanbanBoard.tsx
Apply the typed prop interfaces defined in P2-R2-06 to the new child components
out_of_scope: Adding new kanban features or capabilities — R2 is purely structural
Visual or design changes to the kanban board — styling remains as-is
Decomposing other components — each hotspot has its own task
requirements: KanbanBoard.tsx is decomposed into focused modules with clear single responsibilities
Resulting modules target approximately 200 LOC each unless a larger module preserves better readability
All child components have typed prop interfaces
The composed result produces identical behaviour and rendering to the pre-decomposition KanbanBoard.tsx
acceptance_criteria: KanbanBoard.tsx is decomposed into clearer modules with preserved behaviour
No new features, visual changes, or API changes are introduced
npm run check passes with no new type errors introduced
The kanban board functions identically to the pre-refactor version including drag-and-drop
dependencies: P2-R2-06 — typed prop interfaces for KanbanBoard and its children should be defined
P3-R2-08 — shared hooks (useStages, useSubStages) should be available
P3-R2-09 — apiRequest wrapper should be available if KanbanBoard makes API calls
risks: Drag-and-drop context and state may be tightly coupled to the board component, requiring careful prop drilling or context to preserve behaviour after decomposition
implementation_notes: Natural decomposition boundaries: board layout container, individual columns, individual cards, drag-and-drop event handlers. Consider separating DnD logic into a custom hook (e.g. useKanbanDragDrop) if it is substantial. Keep the top-level KanbanBoard as the composition root.
